content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image by Recall 9When I went on a hunt to find a second-brain app, I had no idea that this field was so vast and the use cases so diverse that it basically became overwhelming. As I kept trying more and more apps, one thing dawned on me: I am not a power user, I don’t want to have conversations with AI agents, and I don’t want them to make changes to my notes on my behalf.
What I wanted was basically a notes app where I could dump all my thoughts, either as text or voice notes, posts that I find on Instagram and X, and links that I find in Chrome so I could later ask AI to find the relevant information for me — not as a message bubble, but as the actual note that I saved.
Unfortunately, most apps, including Mem.ai, Granola.ai, and Recall.it, felt more like a second job than a second-brain app. I just felt overwhelmed by all the technical stuff, including choosing models, connecting MCPs, hiding notes behind agentic conversations, and creating connections, which made me keep looking.
That is when I came across ‘Recall 9’ ( https://recall9.com ). It was basically an obscure and unknown app that nobody was using, but it did exactly what I wanted it to do.
What it really excels at:

• Share posts from Instagram, X, Facebook, or any app for that matter, and it saves the preview, link, text, etc., making it so much easier to search them later.

• Quick audio notes and audio transcription so I can search through them.

• A really simple note editor — that is enough for most users today.

• A Chrome extension so I can save an entire webpage to read later.

• Organization — This was really something that I wanted for a long time. I know AI can organize stuff, but that doesn’t mean we shouldn’t have a say. You can create collections yourself, and then AI just recommends which notes should go to which collection.

• Image transcription — This was truly surprising to me. I saved a few images of posters and watches that I liked, and to my surprise, it didn’t just let me search the text in the images, but it could actually tell whether the images contained a watch or shoes.
Now, the elephant in the room: the price. I found Recall 9 to be cheaper than all the big names, but it’s still $5.99 per month, and many users will still prefer to use Google Keep or Apple Notes for free if they don’t need the AI features. But for me, $5.99 per month is reasonable, given that it does everything I hoped for and has desktop and mobile apps, so I can use it on my Mac as well.
